title: Area-expanding embeddings of rectangles
overall_summary: The paper proves sharp-up-to-constant necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of k-expanding embeddings between n-dimensional rectangles. More generally, it proves lower bounds for the k-dilation of any nonzero-degree map of pairs Œ¶:(U,‚àÇU)->(S,‚àÇS), where U is an open subset of a rectangle R and S is a rectangle. The core proof combines a rectangle-specific relative isoperimetric profile, Almgren-Gromov complexes of cycles, a sweepout/null-homotopy lemma, and a skeleton-by-skeleton tightening construction at an anisotropic scale. For the original problem, the most directly useful results are Estimate 1 and Estimate 2, because they turn a degree-one 2-contracting map between 4-rectangles into product inequalities involving the side lengths; the proof machinery is also useful for deriving variants with mixed products such as S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Estimate 1 (main k-dilation lower bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each dimension n there is a constant c(n)>0 with the following property. Let R=[0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_n] and S=[0,S_1]√ó...√ó[0,S_n] be n-dimensional rectangles with R_1‚â§...‚â§R_n and S_1‚â§...‚â§S_n. Put Q_i=S_i/R_i. Let U be an open subset of R, and let Œ¶:(U,‚àÇU)->(S,‚àÇS) be a map of pairs of positive degree D>0. If 0‚â§j<k‚â§l‚â§n, then the k-dilation of Œ¶ satisfies dil_k(Œ¶) ‚â• c(n) Q_1...Q_j (Q_{j+1}...Q_l)^((k-j)/(l-j)), with the convention that an empty product is 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by homogeneity reduced to the case dil_k(Œ¶)‚â§1, called k-contracting. The case j=0 is proved first by a sweepout argument: subdivide R into blocks [0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_k]√ó[0,Œµ]^{n-k}; the associated complex of cycles C_Œ¶ has very small p-dimensional chains if R_1...R_k is too small compared with S_1...S_k, and the sweepout lemma then makes C_Œ¶ null-homotopic, contradicting nonzero degree. Proposition 4.2 upgrades this to l‚â•k and gives the j=0 part. For 0<j<k<l, Section 5 rewrites the desired estimate for a k-contracting map as [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l ‚â• c(n) S_1...S_l, and reduces to the case where the anisotropic subdivision scale L, defined by R_1...R_j L^{k-j}=Œ¥(n) S_1...S_j S_j^{k-j}, satisfies L‚â§R_{j+1}. Then R is subdivided into fine rectangular blocks of dimensions R_1√ó...√óR_j√óL√ó...√óL. The chain map C_0 associated to Œ¶ is tightened to a new chain map C_1: C_1 agrees with C_0 on the k-skeleton, while each higher-dimensional face is replaced by a filling of its already-defined boundary using the relative isoperimetric inequality for S. This gives |C_1(F^p)|‚â§CŒ¥ S_1...S_j S_j^{p-j} for every fine p-face. The key lemma proves that C_1 has the same degree as C_0, hence D. Next one glues fine faces into a coarser decomposition with blocks R_1√ó...√óR_l√óL√ó...√óL. For a p-face F^p of this coarse complex, the glued complex C_1^+ obeys the volume bound |C_1^+(F^p)|‚â§C(n,Œ¥)[(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l S_j^{p-l}. Since C_1^+ has nonzero degree, the sweepout lemma implies that some p-face has volume at least c(n)S_1...S_p. Comparing the two bounds and using S_j‚â§S_i for i‚â•l gives the desired inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Estimate 2 (degree-refined k-dilation lower bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With R, S, Q_i, U, and Œ¶ as in Estimate 1, for every integer j with 0‚â§j<k, the k-dilation satisfies dil_k(Œ¶) ‚â• c(n) D^((k-j)/(n-j)) Q_1...Q_j (Q_{j+1}...Q_n)^((k-j)/(n-j)), where D>0 is the degree of Œ¶.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Again one reduces to k-contracting maps. For j=0, the n-dilation of a degree D map is at least D Vol(S)/Vol(R)=D Q_1...Q_n, and the linear-algebra monotonicity dil_n^{k/n}‚â§dil_k gives the bound. For 0<j<k, the same anisotropic subdivision and tightening construction used for Estimate 1 is applied with l=n. After gluing, the coarser complex has only the full n-dimensional rectangle R as its top-dimensional face. The tightened and glued n-chain C_1^+(R) represents degree D in H_n(S,‚àÇS), so its volume is at least D S_1...S_n. On the other hand the tightening/gluing estimate gives |C_1^+(R)|‚â§C(n,Œ¥)[(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((n-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_n. Comparing these two inequalities yields [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((n-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_n ‚â• c(n)D S_1...S_n, which is equivalent to the stated dilation lower bound after undoing the normalization.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (necessary conditions for k-expanding embeddings of rectangles)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each dimension n there is a constant c(n)>0 such that the following holds. Let R and S be n-dimensional rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1‚â§...‚â§R_n and S_1‚â§...‚â§S_n. If there is a k-expanding embedding I:S->R, meaning that every k-dimensional surface in S has image volume at least its original volume, then for all integers j,l with 0‚â§j<k‚â§l‚â§n one has (R_1...R_j)^((l-j)/(k-j)) R_{j+1}...R_l ‚â• c(n) (S_1...S_j)^((l-j)/(k-j)) S_{j+1}...S_l.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is obtained from Estimate 1 by taking U=I(S)‚äÇR and Œ¶=I^{-1}:U->S. Since I is k-expanding, its inverse Œ¶ is k-contracting, so dil_k(Œ¶)‚â§1. The map Œ¶ has degree 1 as a map of pairs. Substituting Q_i=S_i/R_i into Estimate 1 and rearranging gives exactly the displayed product inequality. The cases j=0 are the sweepout/cross-sectional volume inequalities, while j>0 are obtained by the tightening construction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (isoperimetric profile of a rectangle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R=[0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_n] be an n-dimensional rectangle with R_1‚â§...‚â§R_n. For a q-dimensional relative integral cycle z in R, define its filling volume to be the least volume of a relative integral (q+1)-chain y in R with ‚àÇy=z, and let I_R^q(V) be the largest filling volume among q-dimensional relative integral cycles in R of volume at most V. For 1‚â§q‚â§n-1 there are constants c(n)>0 and C(n)<‚àû such that if V‚â§c(n)R_1...R_q and V is written uniquely in the form V=c(n) R_1...R_j œÅ^{q-j} for some 0‚â§j‚â§q-1 and some œÅ in the appropriate interval R_j‚â§œÅ‚â§R_{j+1} (with empty product convention and the lower endpoint vacuous when j=0), then I_R^q(V)‚â§C(n) R_1...R_j œÅ^{q-j+1}. In all cases, I_R^q(V)‚â§C(n) R_{q+1} V. The proof also establishes the base case that a relative 0-cycle of volume V can be filled with length at most R_1 V.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the Federer-Fleming deformation theorem and induction on q. For q=0, each point p=(p_1,...,p_n) is joined to the face x_1=0 by the segment [0,p_1]√ó{p_2}√ó...√ó{p_n}, giving filling length at most R_1 times the total multiplicity. For q‚â•1, let z be a q-cycle of volume V. If V‚â§cR_1^q, choose a rectangular lattice of side length L‚âàV^{1/q}‚â§R_1, with ‚àÇR in its (n-1)-skeleton. The deformation lemma moves z to a homologous q-cycle z' in the q-skeleton with |z'|‚â§CV and filling cost ‚â§CLV. With constants chosen so that |z'| is less than the volume of one q-cell, integrality forces z'=0, so z is filled with volume O(V^{(q+1)/q}). If V‚â•cR_1^q, use a lattice of scale L=R_1 arranged so every lattice vertex has first coordinate 0 or R_1. The deformation lemma moves z to a cycle z' in the q-skeleton with filling cost ‚â§CR_1V. Because every interior q-face contains the full [0,R_1] factor, z' has the form [0,R_1]√óz_1, where z_1 is a relative (q-1)-cycle in [0,R_2]√ó...√ó[0,R_n] with volume ‚â§CV/R_1. The induction hypothesis fills z_1 in the lower-dimensional rectangle, and multiplying the filling by [0,R_1] fills z'. Substituting V=cR_1...R_jœÅ^{q-j} gives the small-volume bound C R_1...R_jœÅ^{q-j+1}; the universal bound C R_{q+1}V follows similarly, with the deformation cost absorbed since R_1‚â§R_{q+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.1 (relative Federer-Fleming deformation on a rectangular lattice)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let z be a relative k-cycle in a rectangle R. Suppose R is equipped with a rectangular lattice whose side lengths are all comparable to L, up to a factor of 2, with L‚â§R_1, and suppose ‚àÇR lies in the (n-1)-skeleton of the lattice. Then there is a relative k-cycle z' contained in the k-skeleton of the lattice such that |z'|‚â§C(n)|z| and the relative cycle z'-z has filling volume at most C(n)L|z|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Federer-Fleming deformation is performed one skeleton at a time. Starting with z=z_n, construct cycles z_p lying in the union of the p-skeleton and ‚àÇR, ending at z_k=z'. For each interior p-face F, choose a point x‚ààF and radially project F\{x} to ‚àÇF while fixing ‚àÇF. Averaging over x shows that one can choose x so that the projection stretches p-dimensional volume by at most C(n); the homology swept out has volume at most C(n)L times the mass being projected. Repeating over all p-faces and descending from p=n to p=k gives the stated mass and filling-volume bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Useful special case after Theorem 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R=[0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_n] be an ordered rectangle. If z is a relative p-cycle in R with volume V‚â§c(n) R_1...R_j R_j^{p-j} for some 1‚â§j‚â§p, then z bounds a relative (p+1)-chain y in R with |y|‚â§C(n) R_j V.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 3 to z. In the notation V‚âàR_1...R_iœÅ^{p-i} determined by the theorem, the hypothesis forces the relevant scale œÅ to be at most a constant multiple of R_j. The theorem gives filling volume at most C R_1...R_iœÅ^{p-i+1}=CœÅV, hence at most C R_j V. This is the form used repeatedly in the tightening construction, where boundaries of cells are small enough to be filled at the scale S_j in the target rectangle S.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1 (sweepout/null-homotopy lemma for complexes of cycles)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a constant c(n)>0 such that the following holds. Let S=[0,S_1]√ó...√ó[0,S_n] be an ordered n-rectangle. Let C_0 be a complex of relative integral cycles in S parametrized by a polyhedral complex X; that is, C_0 is a chain map from the cellular chain complex of X to the complex of relative integral Lipschitz chains in S. Suppose C_0(v)=0 for every vertex v of X, and suppose that for every p-dimensional face F^p of X with 1‚â§p‚â§n, the chain C_0(F^p) has volume at most c(n)S_1...S_p. Then C_0 is null-homotopic as a complex of cycles.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let C_1 be the zero complex and build a homotopy C on X√ó[0,1] from C_0 to C_1 skeleton by skeleton. On vertical edges v√ó[0,1], set C=0, which is compatible with the vertex hypothesis. Assume C has been extended up to dimension p-1 while maintaining small-volume bounds. For a p-face, the already-defined boundary is a relative (p-1)-cycle in S of volume at most a small multiple of S_1...S_{p-1}; Theorem 3 fills it with a p-chain of volume at most cS_1...S_p after choosing c(n) sufficiently small. This extends through the n-skeleton. For an (n+1)-face, the boundary is a relative n-cycle of volume less than S_1...S_n, hence represents zero in H_n(S,‚àÇS) because any nonzero multiple of the fundamental class has mass at least Vol(S). Higher-dimensional extensions have no obstruction since H_p(S,‚àÇS)=0 for p‚â•n+1. Thus the original complex is homotopic to zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1 (basic product lower bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be ordered n-rectangles, let Q_i=S_i/R_i, let U‚äÇR be open, and let Œ¶:(U,‚àÇU)->(S,‚àÇS) be a map of nonzero degree D. Then dil_k(Œ¶)‚â•c(n) Q_1...Q_k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By scaling, assume dil_k(Œ¶)‚â§1 and suppose for contradiction that R_1...R_k<cS_1...S_k. Subdivide R into blocks congruent to [0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_k]√ó[0,Œµ]^{n-k}, using a fine grid only in the last n-k directions. Let C_Œ¶ be the associated complex of cycles, C_Œ¶(F)=Œ¶(F‚à©U). Any face of dimension p<k lies in ‚àÇR and maps to zero. Each k-face has volume at most R_1...R_k, so its image has volume less than cS_1...S_k. For p>k, the linear algebra corollary implies Œ¶ is also p-contracting, so the image of a p-face has volume at most R_1...R_k Œµ^{p-k}; choosing Œµ sufficiently small makes these volumes less than cS_1...S_p. Lemma 4.1 then makes C_Œ¶ null-homotopic, hence degree zero, contradicting the hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2 (j=0 estimates and degree-volume bound)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let l‚â•k. If U‚äÇR and Œ¶:(U,‚àÇU)->(S,‚àÇS) has nonzero degree D, then dil_k(Œ¶)‚â•c(n)(Q_1...Q_l)^{k/l}. Also dil_k(Œ¶)‚â•(|D|Q_1...Q_n)^{k/n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 4.1 applied with k replaced by l, the l-dilation is at least c(n)Q_1...Q_l. Lemma 8.1 gives dil_l(Œ¶)^{k/l}‚â§dil_k(Œ¶), proving the first inequality. For the second, the n-dilation must be at least |D|Vol(S)/Vol(R)=|D|Q_1...Q_n, since the image n-volume counted with multiplicity has degree |D|. Applying the same linear-algebra monotonicity from n to k gives dil_k(Œ¶)‚â•(|D|Q_1...Q_n)^{k/n}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5 algebraic reduction and choice of tightening scale L
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the nontrivial cases of Estimates 1 and 2, one may assume Œ¶ is k-contracting, 0<j<k<l, and it suffices to prove [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l ‚â• c(n)S_1...S_l and [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((n-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_n ‚â• c(n)|D|S_1...S_n. For fixed j define L by R_1...R_j L^{k-j}=Œ¥(n)S_1...S_j S_j^{k-j}, where Œ¥(n)>0 is small. It is enough to prove the estimates under the additional assumption L‚â§R_{j+1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inequalities can be rewritten as [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^{1/(k-j)} ‚â• c(n)[(S_1...S_l)/(R_1...R_l)]^{1/(l-k)} and [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^{1/(k-j)} ‚â• c(n)[|D|(S_1...S_n)/(R_1...R_n)]^{1/(n-k)}. The right-hand sides do not depend on j, so one chooses j, among 1‚â§j‚â§k-1, minimizing the left-hand side. If j=k-1, then L=Œ¥ S_1...S_{k-1}S_{k-1}/(R_1...R_{k-1})‚â§Œ¥ S_1...S_k/(R_1...R_{k-1}); Proposition 4.1 gives R_1...R_k‚â•cS_1...S_k, so choosing Œ¥ small yields L‚â§R_k=R_{j+1}. If j<k-1, minimality compared with j+1 gives [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^{1/(k-j)}‚â§[(R_1...R_{j+1})/(S_1...S_{j+1})]^{1/(k-j-1)}. Raising to the product of exponents and rearranging gives S_1...S_j S_{j+1}^{k-j}‚â§R_1...R_j R_{j+1}^{k-j}. Since S_j‚â§S_{j+1} and R_1...R_jL^{k-j}=Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}, it follows that L‚â§R_{j+1} after absorbing Œ¥.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 6 tightening construction and volume estimate (V)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume Œ¶:(U,‚àÇU)->(S,‚àÇS) is k-contracting, 0<j<k<l‚â§n, and the scale L defined by R_1...R_jL^{k-j}=Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{k-j} satisfies L‚â§R_{j+1}. Subdivide R into fine blocks of dimensions R_1√ó...√óR_j√óL√ó...√óL and construct the tightened complex C_1 by filling higher-dimensional boundaries in S. If the fine complex is glued into a coarser complex B^+ whose blocks have dimensions R_1√ó...√óR_l√óL√ó...√óL, then for every p-face F^p of B^+ with p‚â•l the glued chain satisfies |C_1^+(F^p)|‚â§C(n,Œ¥)[(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l S_j^{p-l}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The fine k-faces have volume at most R_1...R_jL^{k-j}=Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}; because Œ¶ is k-contracting, their images have this same upper bound. Define C_1=C_0 on the k-skeleton. For a (k+1)-face, C_1 of its boundary has volume O(Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}); by the special isoperimetric bound in S it fills with volume O(Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{k+1-j}). Inductively, if all p-faces have volume O(Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}), then the boundary of a (p+1)-face has the same order of volume and fills with volume O(Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{p+1-j}). Thus every fine p-face obeys |C_1(F^p)|‚â§CŒ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}. A coarse p-face is a union of N=(R_{j+1}/L)...(R_l/L) fine p-faces, so the glued mass is at most CŒ¥N S_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}. Substituting L^{k-j}=Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}/(R_1...R_j) and simplifying gives the displayed bound, with powers of Œ¥ absorbed into C(n,Œ¥).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1 (homotopy between small complexes agreeing on a skeleton)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is Œµ(n)>0 such that the following holds. Let C_0 and C_1 be chain maps from the cellular chain complex of a polyhedral complex X to the relative integral Lipschitz chains in an ordered n-rectangle S. Suppose C_0 and C_1 agree on the k-skeleton of X. Suppose further that for every p-face F^p of X with k+1‚â§p‚â§n, both |C_0(F^p)| and |C_1(F^p)| are at most Œµ(n)S_1...S_p. Then C_0 and C_1 are homotopic as complexes of cycles.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Construct a homotopy C on X√ó[0,1]. For p‚â§k set C(F^p√ó[0,1])=0, using the fact that C_0 and C_1 agree on those faces. Inductively, suppose C has been constructed on lower-dimensional faces with bounds |C(F^p√ó[0,1])|‚â§cS_1...S_{p+1}. For a p-face F^p with p‚â•k+1, the boundary to be filled is C((‚àÇF^p)√ó[0,1])+C_1(F^p)-C_0(F^p), a p-cycle whose volume is at most a small multiple of S_1...S_p. Theorem 3 fills it by a (p+1)-chain of volume at most cS_1...S_{p+1}. This extends to the n-skeleton. For n-faces, the boundary n-cycle has volume less than Vol(S) and is therefore null-homologous; higher extensions have no obstruction because H_q(S,‚àÇS)=0 for q‚â•n+1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Key Lemma in Section 6 (tightening preserves degree)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Section 6 setup, let C_0 be the complex of cycles associated to Œ¶ on the fine subdivision B of R, so C_0(F)=Œ¶(F‚à©U). Let C_1 be the tightened complex that agrees with C_0 on faces of dimension at most k and whose higher-dimensional faces are chosen by the isoperimetric filling procedure described above. Then degree(C_1)=degree(Œ¶)=degree(C_0).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The issue is that C_1 is obtained by noncanonical fillings and need not visibly be homotopic to C_0. Guth proves degree preservation by gradual tightening. Let B_s be the finer subdivision with block dimensions R_1√ó...√óR_j√ó2^{-s}L√ó...√ó2^{-s}L. Define Œì_s:B_s->I_rel(S) by setting Œì_s(F)=Œ¶(F‚à©U) for dim F‚â§k and filling all higher faces by the same isoperimetric tightening rule. Thus Œì_0=C_1. Let Œì_{s+1}^+ be Œì_{s+1} glued back to the coarser subdivision B_s. The complexes Œì_s and Œì_{s+1}^+ agree on the k-skeleton, and both have sufficiently small higher-dimensional face volumes of order Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}‚â§Œ¥S_1...S_p; Lemma 6.1 gives a homotopy, so their degrees are equal. Since gluing does not change degree, degree Œì_s is independent of s. For large s, define Œ≤_s(F)=Œ¶(F‚à©U) on every face of B_s. Because p-faces with p‚â•k+1 have volumes R_1...R_jL^{p-j}2^{-(p-j)s}, choosing s large makes all Œ≤_s(F) small enough for Lemma 6.1. Œ≤_s and Œì_s agree on the k-skeleton and have small higher faces, so they are homotopic. But Œ≤_s is the original map-induced complex and has degree degree(Œ¶). Therefore Œì_0=C_1 has the same degree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.1 and Corollary 8.1 (higher-dimensional dilation controlled by k-dilation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a linear map between Euclidean spaces and let |Œõ^mL| denote its m-dimensional dilation, the operator norm on m-vectors. If l>k, then |Œõ^lL|^{k/l}‚â§|Œõ^kL|. Consequently, if Œ¶ is a smooth map with k-dilation D(k) and l-dilation D(l), then for l‚â•k one has D(l)^{k/l}‚â§D(k). In particular, a k-contracting map is also l-contracting for every l‚â•k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the singular values of L in nondecreasing order 0‚â§s_1‚â§...‚â§s_m, where m is the smaller dimension. Then |Œõ^rL| is the product of the r largest singular values, s_{m-r+1}...s_m. The geometric mean of the l largest singular values is at most the geometric mean of the k largest singular values, which is exactly |Œõ^lL|^{1/l}‚â§|Œõ^kL|^{1/k}. Raising to the k-th power gives the lemma. Taking the supremum over points for dŒ¶ gives the corollary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.2 (linear expansion/contraction interpolation inequality)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If j‚â§k‚â§l and L is a linear map between Euclidean spaces, then |Œõ^jL|^((l-k)/(l-j)) |Œõ^lL|^((k-j)/(l-j)) ‚â§ |Œõ^kL|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let s_1‚â§...‚â§s_m be the singular values. Then |Œõ^rL| is the product of the r largest singular values. Raising the desired inequality to the power l-j, the left-hand side becomes (s_{m-j+1}...s_m)^{l-k}(s_{m-l+1}...s_m)^{k-j}. Reorganizing separates the largest j singular values and the intermediate singular values. Since the singular values appearing among the largest k dominate those appearing farther down among the largest l, the product is bounded by (s_{m-k+1}...s_m)^{l-j}=|Œõ^kL|^{l-j}. Taking (l-j)-th roots gives the result. Guth notes that this has no direct pointwise-to-global analogue for nonlinear maps, and the paper's tightening construction serves as a nonlinear substitute at the level of complexes of cycles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2 (sufficient conditions for k-expanding embeddings; sharpness up to constants)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each dimension n there is a constant C(n)>0 such that the following holds. Let R and S be ordered n-rectangles. If for every 0‚â§j<k‚â§l‚â§n one has (R_1...R_j)^((l-j)/(k-j)) R_{j+1}...R_l ‚â• C(n)(S_1...S_j)^((l-j)/(k-j)) S_{j+1}...S_l, then there exists a k-expanding embedding from S into R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction composes a linear k-expanding part with foldings. First, a folding lemma says that if all partial products R_1...R_p are sufficiently larger than S_1...S_p, then there is a 1-expanding embedding S->R; this is built by repeatedly using two-dimensional foldings in coordinate pairs, where a long thin rectangle is folded into a shorter wider one without contracting lengths. Second, an algebraic lemma says that the displayed inequalities imply the existence of a k-contracting linear diffeomorphism from R to a rectangle T such that T_1...T_p‚â•S_1...S_p for every p. The proof of the algebraic lemma iteratively modifies R by linear maps that enlarge the first q axes by Œª_q and shrink the remaining axes by Œª_q^{-q/(k-q)}, preserving k-contraction, until the first deficient partial product is corrected; then it uses induction on the remaining dimensions. Taking the inverse of the k-contracting linear map gives a k-expanding linear map T->R, and composing S->T with T->R gives the desired k-expanding embedding.

proof_techniques.name: Normalize by scaling the target metric
proof_techniques.description: The k-dilation estimates are homogeneous. In proofs Guth often assumes the map is k-contracting, dil_k‚â§1, and proves a side-length inequality; the general lower bound for dil_k follows by rescaling the target rectangle so that the normalized map becomes k-contracting and then undoing the scaling.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a hypothesis Dil_2(f)‚â§1 no rescaling is needed, so the paper's k-contracting inequalities can be applied directly with k=2. If one derives an intermediate estimate with another dilation normalization, scaling keeps track of the correct powers of side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Rectangle-specific relative isoperimetric profile
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 estimates fillings of relative integral cycles in a rectangle in terms of the side lengths. The proof uses Federer-Fleming deformation at the smallest relevant scale and induction after splitting off the shortest interval factor. The profile reflects anisotropy: a cycle of size about R_1...R_jœÅ^{p-j} fills with size about R_1...R_jœÅ^{p-j+1}, and large cycles have a linear filling bound C R_{p+1}V.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The target in the original problem is a rectangle S, so this isoperimetric profile can be used to replace pieces of f-slices by cheaper fillings whose cost depends on S_1,S_2,S_3,S_4. The mixed exponents in the desired inequality are likely to come from choosing the filling scale between two target side lengths.

proof_techniques.name: Complexes of cycles as discrete sweepouts
proof_techniques.description: A complex of cycles is a chain map from the face complex of a parameter polyhedron X to the relative integral chains of S. It records, for each p-face of X, a p-chain in S, with boundaries matching. A map of pairs Œ¶:(U,‚àÇU)->(S,‚àÇS) and a polyhedral decomposition of R naturally produce such a complex by sending each face F to Œ¶(F‚à©U). The complex has a well-defined degree via the induced map on H_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Instead of working with a continuous family of preimage or image slices, one can decompose the domain rectangle and use the resulting chain map. This is robust under piecewise smooth maps and under noncontinuous choices of fillings during tightening.

proof_techniques.name: Small-volume sweepout null-homotopy
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.1 says that if every p-face in a complex of cycles has volume below cS_1...S_p, then the complex is null-homotopic. The proof constructs a homotopy to zero by filling boundaries skeleton-by-skeleton using the rectangle isoperimetric inequality; the only top-dimensional obstruction is avoided because an n-cycle of volume less than Vol(S) cannot represent the fundamental class.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A degree-one map gives a non-null complex. Therefore, after any tightening procedure that preserves degree, some face must still have volume at least cS_1...S_p. Comparing that forced lower bound with an upper bound from Dil_2‚â§1 is the main route to side-length inequalities.

proof_techniques.name: Anisotropic subdivision at a critical scale L
proof_techniques.description: For 0<j<k, Guth subdivides the domain rectangle into blocks R_1√ó...√óR_j√óL√ó...√óL, where L is chosen by R_1...R_jL^{k-j}=Œ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{k-j}. This makes the k-dimensional faces small enough in the target to start the isoperimetric tightening process at the scale S_j. Section 5 gives algebraic conditions ensuring L‚â§R_{j+1}, so this subdivision fits the ordering of the domain side lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For n=4 and k=2, the nontrivial choice is j=1, so one should examine blocks with one short R_1 direction and one adjustable L direction. This is the mechanism in the paper that produces inequalities involving powers of R_1/S_1.

proof_techniques.name: Skeleton-by-skeleton tightening
proof_techniques.description: After subdividing, C_1 is defined to equal the original map-induced complex on the k-skeleton. For each (k+1)-face, the already-defined boundary is a small k-cycle in S and is replaced by a filling from the rectangle isoperimetric inequality. Then the same step is repeated for (k+2)-faces and so on. The result is a new complex whose p-face volumes are bounded by CŒ¥S_1...S_jS_j^{p-j}, often much smaller than the original images of p-faces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can bypass the obstacle that Dil_2 controls only 2-dimensional areas directly: by tightening, one manufactures controlled 3- and 4-dimensional chains from the controlled 2-skeleton.

proof_techniques.name: Gradual tightening to preserve degree
proof_techniques.description: Because the chosen fillings in C_1 are not canonically related to the original chains, degree preservation is nontrivial. Guth introduces finer subdivisions B_s with scale 2^{-s}L and constructs tightened complexes Œì_s. Consecutive Œì_s are compared after gluing by a homotopy lemma for small complexes, and for very fine s the original map-induced complex Œ≤_s has small higher-dimensional faces and is homotopic to Œì_s. This proves the tightened complex has the same degree as the original map.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any proof that replaces f-images of cells by cheaper fillings must include a degree-preservation argument. The gradual-tightening method is a ready-made way to justify that the resulting tightened sweepout still detects degree one.

proof_techniques.name: Gluing fine cells into coarser l-dimensional blocks
proof_techniques.description: After tightening on the fine subdivision, Guth regroups fine faces into coarser blocks R_1√ó...√óR_l√óL√ó...√óL. A coarse p-face contains N=(R_{j+1}/L)...(R_l/L) fine p-faces, so its mass is bounded by N times the fine mass. Substituting the defining equation for L yields the key upper bound with factor [(R_1...R_j)/(S_1...S_j)]^((l-k)/(k-j)) R_1...R_l.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Different choices of l produce different product inequalities. In dimension 4 with k=2, gluing at l=3 and l=4 gives the paper's main anisotropic constraints, which can be combined with side ordering and additional hypotheses.

proof_techniques.name: Linear-algebra dilation monotonicity and interpolation
proof_techniques.description: The singular-value identities show that if a map is k-contracting then it is l-contracting for every l‚â•k, and more generally for linear maps |Œõ^jL|^((l-k)/(l-j))|Œõ^lL|^((k-j)/(l-j))‚â§|Œõ^kL|. The nonlinear theorem is presented as an analogue of this expansion/contraction tradeoff after replacing the map by a tightened complex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Dil_2‚â§1 automatically controls 3- and 4-dimensional volumes of individual smooth images, which is useful for the j=0 estimates. The interpolation inequality also explains why exponents such as 1/2 and 3/2 may arise when combining 2-dimensional control with lower-dimensional squeezing.

proof_techniques.name: Degree as a top-dimensional mass lower bound
proof_techniques.description: If a relative n-chain in S represents degree D times the fundamental class, then its mass is at least |D|Vol(S). This simple observation is used in Estimate 2 after tightening and gluing all the way to l=n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree-one map between 4-rectangles, any tightened 4-chain still representing the fundamental class must have mass at least S_1S_2S_3S_4. This supplies a hard lower bound to compare against tightened upper bounds.

other_useful_info: The paper gives model examples showing Theorem 3 is close to sharp: cycles [0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_j]√óS^{q-j}(œÅ) have volume about R_1...R_jœÅ^{q-j} and filling volume about R_1...R_jœÅ^{q-j+1}, while M copies of [0,R_1]√ó...√ó[0,R_q]√ó{p} have filling volume about R_{q+1} times their volume. Guth notes that the arguments depend mainly on knowing the target's relative isoperimetric profile, so analogous estimates can be attempted for other targets with known profiles. Appendix 2 states product-type generalizations and ellipsoid corollaries, while the main references for the sweepout technology are Almgren's complexes of cycles, Gromov's filling/sweepout estimates, and Federer-Fleming deformation theory.
why_relevant: Although stated for k-expanding embeddings, this paper contains Guth's rectangle-specific isoperimetric profile estimates, complexes of cycles, and tightening construction. These are exactly the tools needed to prove anisotropic inequalities involving products of side lengths such as S_1 S_2^{1/2} S_3^{3/2} S_4.
